agents.name: Hero
agents.role: Trusted Chief of Staff for Hero (Reggie) — strategic thinking, coordination, and decision support across Mirror App and future products. Thinks before building. Use proactively on "should we build", "help me think through", "prioritize", "what are my options", "chief of staff", "cos".

agents.name: Taylor
agents.role: Customer success specialist. Use proactively for tester feedback analysis, churn signal detection, behavioral pattern synthesis, and tester communications.

agents.name: Elon
agents.role: Data analysis specialist. Use proactively for Plaid transaction patterns, retention metrics, behavioral data, user engagement, and quantitative analysis.

agents.name: Gil
agents.role: Forensic debugger and root cause investigator. Use when a bug resists 3 fix attempts, when root cause is unknown, when reproduction is inconsistent, when the Developer escalates, or when Hero invokes directly for any debugging task. Finds the problem — does not write the fix.

agents.name: Tony
agents.role: Expert software developer. Use proactively when writing code, debugging, reviewing code, building features, or solving technical problems across any project.

agents.name: Tucker
agents.role: Reviews any output for hallucinated claims, fabricated statistics, and unsupported assertions. Use for investor materials, regulatory content, published docs, or any high-stakes deliverable.

agents.name: Warren
agents.role: Financial strategy specialist. Use proactively for pricing models, unit economics, runway projections, revenue analysis, and monetization decisions.

agents.name: infra
agents.role: Infrastructure specialist for SSH key management, multi-machine git workflows, cron/launchd scheduling, and server configuration. Use when setting up remote access, diagnosing multi-machine git conflicts, debugging cron/launchd failures, or fixing server environment issues.

agents.name: Johnny
agents.role: Legal and compliance specialist for fintech and behavioral wellness. Use proactively for privacy, Plaid compliance, financial data regulations, terms of service, and user consent.

agents.name: Sky
agents.role: Marketing orchestrator for Mirror. Use proactively for copy, content, campaigns, conversions, email sequences, SEO, and any marketing execution task.

agents.name: Bruce
agents.role: Autonomous nightly system reviewer. Analyzes performance logs, runs canary evals, detects agent degradation, and drafts improvement proposals for Hero's morning review. Read-only — never modifies system files.

agents.name: Ye
agents.role: Product strategy specialist. Use proactively for feature validation, roadmap prioritization, product-market fit analysis, and build/don't-build decisions.

agents.name: research
agents.role: Market intelligence and research specialist. Use proactively for trend scanning, competitor monitoring, social signals, user language mining, and raw intelligence gathering.

agents.name: reviewer
agents.role: Code quality reviewer. Runs after developer completes implementation. Tests outputs against sprint contract acceptance criteria.

selfImprovement.text: Presented full metrics + strategy plan without asking the foundational scoping question: 'what does the existing toolset (X native analytics) already solve, and what's the minimum new work needed to close the gap?' Resulted in ~60% of Plan v1 being discarded after Hero pushed back
selfImprovement.context: 38twelveDaily strategy enforcement — first plan included metrics pipeline + dashboard that got fully dropped

selfImprovement.text: Hook demands verification of functions/files created earlier in the same session, costing turns on ceremony comments for things the agent demonstrably just wrote. 5+ turns this session spent writing 'verified at file:line' notes for self-authored code
selfImprovement.context: PreToolUse hook enforces verification but can't distinguish 'never seen' from 'just wrote'. Proposed fix: auto-pass when target file mtime is within session window, OR honor an inline marker like // pre-tool-verified

selfImprovement.text: PreToolUse + stop hooks evaluate atomically without memory/prior-turn visibility — caused 5+ stop-hook flags and 2+ pretool hook retries in a single session. Hero rated the session progressed-but-spun-wheels specifically because of hook friction

C0 phase emitted a ledger entry with summary="No summary extracted" and category="unknown" — parser silently failed on one of 4 proposals in the LLM output but still recorded a stub. Purged as P-2026-04-16-006 during review.
selfImprovement.context: c0-proposals.sh extract/parse is fragile when LLM output varies in formatting

selfImprovement.text: Scope round-trip on metrics dashboard (planned then stripped), pretooluse hook over-fired on self-authored references → 5 lessons saved

Hook architecture friction: stop hook + pretool hook evaluate atomically without memory/prior-turn context visibility — caused 5+ false positives on memory-grounded citations and 2+ pretool retries on already-verified endpoints. Hero rated progressed-but-spun-wheels because of this. Bruce proposed source-labeling convention (P-2026-04-16-001, pending). → 3 lessons saved

** Tonight's D0 briefing shows an **empty Eval Canaries table** despite B1 having produced a confirmed chief-of-staff TC
** Tonight's A1 metrics report **`capability_gaps_7d: 8`** — 8 instances in the past 7 days where an agent explicitly
** The chief-of-staff has **one canary eval: TC1 (Feature Prioritization)**. Tonight TC1 scored 4.6, with C2 (inter-opti
[object Object] RSS parser bug took 3 iterations in main thread — debug agent's root-cause discipline would have isolated faster
Destination checkboxes shipped hidden inside collapsed edit panel — reviewer would have caught the UX miss pre-deploy
Piecewise discovery of pipeline structure (ls, grep, read, ls, grep...) — Explore would have mapped in one call

Hero feedback: 'less mistakes, spawning the rights agents, and the actual system we built working in full for once'. Root cause = discipline gap, not capability gap. System has debug/reviewer/Explore agents + triggers rule but no enforcement mechanism. Candidate for Bruce proposal: pre-commit hook or stop-hook check that fails if deploying code without a reviewer pass.

Many preventable bugs shipped and fixed mid-session: [object Object] in DB, subrequest cap blowout requiring chunking refactor, stale archive flood needing purge, invisible checkboxes requiring redeploy, non-clickable cards requiring redeploy. Zero subagents spawned in a 4hr build. → 6 lessons saved
All 3 fix attempts were caused by not verifying external dependencies before coding. Updated code-accuracy.md rule.

Hero had to manually catch 3 inline loop failures: agent deployment summary not presented, surprises not logged inline, fixes not applied inline. The rules existed but were not followed. This is the 2nd+ session where Hero catches what the system should catch automatically.

3 deploy failures from unverified external dependencies (model ID, RSS URLs, JSON parsing). Hero caught 3 inline loop failures (agent summary, surprise logging, inline fixes not triggered automatically). → 4 lessons saved

Stop hook caught 4 unverified-completion claims; pretooluse hook blocked 8 edits until I verified functions/endpoints/URLs — discipline slowed momentum but prevented fabricated output → 3 lessons saved
3 accuracy violations caught by stop hook and Hero directly. Both subagent calls returned empty. Verify-before-ship repeated from prior session. → 5 lessons saved
Vercel GitHub auto-deploy has silently broken 3-4 times across sessions — git push succeeds but Vercel webhook does not fire, leaving live dashboard stale while script claims success

Session-briefing hook does not inject durable corrections — facts Hero has had to repeat across multiple sessions. Two recurring examples: (1) Bruce is /bruce slash command not cron, (2) Vercel GitHub auto-deploy is broken, use CLI. Memory files for both exist but are not auto-loaded at session start, so each new session begins with a stale mental model until Hero corrects it. This is the core OS problem — recurring corrections should be prevented by injection, not re-learned by correction.

Claimed Bruce 3 AM cron would populate evals AND that Vercel auto-deploy would pick up git push — both wrong, corrected by Hero. Root cause: stale mental model at session start + no enforcement of verification-before-claim. Fixed systemically via Stop hook check 7 + session-briefing pinned corrections. → 6 lessons saved

Create infra agent
Library reorganization baseline — measure against this in 2 weeks

Skill gap detection cant see agent files — re-flags addressed gaps as open
Stop hook infinite loop: evaluates full transcript history, re-flags corrected historical violation on every new response. Hook should scope evaluation to current response only.
Stop hook evaluated full transcript instead of latest response — caused infinite rejection loop after a corrected violation. Self-corrected by scoping hook prompt to latest response only.
Stop hook entered infinite loop re-flagging corrected violations — scoped to latest response. PreToolUse hook blocked edits for newly created files. → 0 lessons saved
4 PreToolUse hook catches on developer (API verification), 5 Stop hook catches on chief-of-staff (false certainty) → 3 lessons saved
LinkedIn returns 999 on WebFetch — asked for screenshots instead → 3 lessons saved

findings: WATCH: Friction Rate — 3 fix_attempts in 14 days (red line at 6)
nightShift.briefingContent:
# Night Shift — 2026-04-16

## Status: YELLOW

## Key Findings
- WATCH: Friction Rate — 3 fix_attempts in 14 days (red line at 6)

## What Hero Worked On (7-day)
- cloudflare-workers: 4 sessions
- css: 4 sessions
- d1: 4 sessions
- nextjs: 4 sessions
- agents: 3 sessions

## Agent Usage (7-day)
- developer: 2 sessions
- explore: 2 sessions
- research: 2 sessions
- reviewer: 2 sessions
- chief-of-staff: 1 sessions

## Eval Canaries
| Agent | Score | Baseline | Delta | Status |
|-------|-------|----------|-------|--------|
| chief-of-staff | 4.6 | 5.0 | -0.40 | ok |

## Skill Gaps
None detected

## Memory Health
87 total files, 0 consolidation candidates

## Friction Trends (7-day)
- fix_attempts: 3
- escalations: 0
- hook_catches: 0
- re_explanations: 0
- patterns_detected: 4

## Proposals
I now have everything I need. Here are tonight's proposals:

---

# Proposals — 2026-04-16

---

## P-2026-04-16-006

**Category:** `agent_methodology`

**Problem:** Chief-of-staff TC1 scored 4.6/5.0 (baseline 5.0, delta −0.4). C2 was specifically cited: *"T&C dependency (legal/Plaid blocker) is embedded in the option breakdown rather than surfaced at the top before merit comparison, per the agent's own multi-option instruction."*

The instruction is already present in `~/.claude/agents/chief-of-staff.md`:
> *"If one option is a hard blocker or prerequisite for another, say so at the top — before comparing merit. Dependency framing is not a footnote."*

The rule failed in practice despite being in the file. This is a structural compliance problem, not a missing rule. When the agent processes a multi-option prompt, the cognitive default is to analyze each option in parallel — which naturally buries blockers inside option bodies. Text instructions describing *what* to do don't override that generation pattern as reliably as a required *template* specifying *how the output must be structured*. The fix is to replace the prose instruction with a concrete required output skeleton.

**Solution:** In `~/.claude/agents/chief-of-staff.md`, under `## Multi-Option Recommendations`, add a required response template immediately after the "Inter-option dependencies" bullet:

```markdown
**Required format for any multi-option response:**

> **⚠️ Blockers & Dependencies** *(skip section if none)*
> [Hard blockers, legal requirements, or sequencing constraints that change the comparison]
> 
> **Option A — [Name]**
> Recommendation: [yes/no/conditional]
> Opportunity cost: [one vivid sentence on what you give up]
> 
> **Option B — [Name]**
> ...
> 
> **Recommendation:** [single option] — [one-line reason]
```

The section header `⚠️ Blockers & Dependencies` appearing *before* any option heading forces structural compliance — the agent must populate or explicitly skip the section before it reaches option analysis.

**Impact:** C2 and the structural compliance gap are addressed at the format layer, not the instruction layer. TC1 expected to return to 5.0. The template also standardizes CoS multi-option responses, making them easier for Hero to scan at a glance.

**Risk:** Low. The template is additive and scoped only to the multi-option case. The risk is verbosity if Hero asks for a quick informal comparison — mitigated by the `(skip section if none)` annotation on the blockers header and the existing `## Communication Style` guardrail against preamble.

---

## P-2026-04-16-007

**Category:** `self_improvement`

**Problem:** Tonight I received `capability_gaps_7d: 9` — a single opaque integer. This is the highest-signal metric in tonight's data (9 gaps in 7 sessions = >1 gap per session), yet I cannot generate a targeted proposal because I don't know which agent triggered each gap, in which domain, or whether any gap type has recurred 3+ times (the threshold that would trigger Gap Diagnosis Protocol).

P-2026-04-16-004 (accepted earlier tonight) fixed B3's analysis — it now ingests raw capability_gap events from the performance log. But B3 is an LLM-over-text step that runs during the night and produces prose findings. By the time I'm writing proposals, I only see the count in the A1 metrics payload.

Separately, tonight's dynamic evals (DYN-1, DYN-2) were drawn from `cloudflare-workers,css` domain sessions — confirming that the capability gaps and the high-activity domain cluster are related. Without per-agent gap attribution in the metrics, I can't connect these two signals in a single proposal.

**Solution:** In `~/.claude/night-shift/phases/a1-log-analysis.sh`, after emitting `capability_gaps_7d`, add a second metric emit for a structured gap attribution summary:

```bash
# After the existing capability_gaps_7d emit:
GAP_ATTR=$(grep '"event":"capability_gap"' "$PERF_LOG" 2>/dev/null | \
  jq -rsc --arg cutoff "$SEVEN_DAYS_AGO" \
    '[.[] | select(.date >= $cutoff)] |
     group_by(.agent) |
     map({agent: .[0].agent, count: length,
          top_domain: (group_by(.context) | max_by(length) | .[0].context // "unknown")})' \
  2>/dev/null || echo "[]")

emit_event "{\"phase\":\"a1\",\"type\":\"data\",\"key\":\"capability_gap_attribution\",\"value\":$GAP_ATTR}"
```

Then pass `capability_gap_attribution` alongside `capability_gaps_7d` in the C0 (proposal generation) data payload — same as how `domain_frequency_7d` and `agent_usage_7d` are currently passed.

**Impact:** Tonight's 9 gaps become *"developer: 6 gaps (cloudflare-workers), research: 2 gaps (unknown), reviewer: 1 gap (css)"* — or whatever the actual breakdown is. I can generate a targeted `skill_gap` or `agent_methodology` proposal for the high-count agent instead of a generic observation. The gap metric goes from decorative to actionable on the night it spikes.

**Risk:** Medium. Depends on capability_gap events having a consistent `agent` field in the performance log. If the field is absent or misnamed, the jq produces an empty array — same as today, no regression. If `$PERF_LOG` has no capability_gap events at all (e.g., event naming changes), the emit produces `[]` and the proposal context is no worse than tonight.

---

## P-2026-04-16-008

**Category:** `eval_evolution`

**Problem:** TC1 raw scores: `{c1:5, c2:4, c3:5, c4:4, c5:5}`. Two criteria were docked. The eval notes explain only C2 in detail. C4 (opportunity cost analysis — *"explicitly names what gets delayed by each choice"*) was also docked to 4, but the notes say only *"Strong performance across the board"* without identifying what was missing in C4.

Night Shift's proposal process is evidence-driven: without a one-line note on why C4 was docked, I can't generate a targeted fix. The current eval entry gives me the score, the criterion text, and the rubric — but not which specific output behavior fell short. For C4, I don't know if the agent: (a) named opportunity cost for only the recommended option and not all options, (b) used abstract language instead of concrete tradeoffs, or (c) was docked for a different reason entirely. Each diagnosis leads to a different AOP change.

This blind spot compounds over time: as the eval suite grows and criteria multiply, any undocumented docking becomes a ghost regression — visible in the score, invisible in the diagnosis.

**Solution:** In `~/.claude/agents/evals/chief-of-staff-evals.md`, add the following line to the **Scoring Rubric** section:

```markdown
**Required:** For any criterion scored below 5, write one sentence explaining what was missing
or wrong in the agent's response. Format: `C[N] docked: [reason].`
Undocumented dockings are invalid — rescore or add a note before logging results.
```

Additionally, update the **Score Tracker** template row to include a notes column structured for per-criterion explanations:

```markdown
| YYYY-MM-DD | model | prompt# | C1 | C2 | C3 | C4 | C5 | avg | C2 docked: [reason]. C4 docked: [reason]. |
```

**Impact:** Every future eval run produces a complete diagnostic record. Night Shift gains a full failure inventory per run — not just the most prominent regression. The C4 mystery tonight becomes a specific, actionable finding tomorrow. Applies to all chief-of-staff evals and, when replicated, to all agent eval files.

**Risk:** Low. The rubric change adds friction to the scoring step — the evaluator must write notes for docked criteria rather than just logging numbers. This is the desired friction: undocumented regressions are more expensive than the 30 seconds it takes to note them.

---

## P-2026-04-16-009

**Category:** `skill_gap`

**Problem:** Sessions in the last 7 days are concentrated in `cloudflare-workers` (4 sessions) and `d1` (4 sessions). Dynamic evals DYN-1 and DYN-2 — drawn directly from recent session work — are both tagged `cloudflare-workers,css`. `capability_gaps_7d` is 9. `fix_attempts_7d` is 3.

Cloudflare Workers and D1 have hard runtime constraints that differ from standard Node.js/Postgres environments that agents' training skews toward:

| Constraint | Workers/D1 behavior | Standard Node assumption |
|---|---|---|
| Runtime APIs | No `fs`, `net`, `crypto` (Node.js built-ins blocked) | Node.js globals available |
| Response model | `Response` object required; `res.send()` doesn't exist | Express-style response |
| D1 SQL dialect | SQLite semantics; no `RETURNING` in older D1 versions; batch API required for multi-statement | Postgres `RETURNING` works |
| Bundle size | 1MB compressed limit on Worker scripts | No relevant limit |
| Cold start | `waitUntil()` for background tasks; awaiting after response is dropped | Async after response is valid |

Mirror avoided equivalent friction with `.claude/rules/xano-rules.md` — 15 critical rules codifying patterns that caused real bugs. That file is cited directly in the mirror-developer agent as a required pre-flight read. The Cloudflare stack has no equivalent.

**Staleness check:** `cloudflare-workers` and `d1` sessions: 4 each in the past 7 days. Evidence is current. This is not a new agent proposal — it's a rules file for an existing developer agent context.

**Solution:** Create `~/Claude-Projects/[cloudflare-project]/.claude/rules/cloudflare-rules.md` with the following structure:

```markdown
# Cloudflare Workers + D1 — Critical Rules

## Workers Runtime
- Rule 1: No Node.js built-in modules (fs, net, path, crypto). Use Web APIs only.
- Rule 2: Response must be a `Response` object — never call `res.send()` or `res.json()`.
- Rule 3: Background work after response: use `ctx.waitUntil(promise)`. Any async work
  awaited after `return new Response(...)` is silently dropped.
- Rule 4: Bundle size limit is 1MB compressed. Flag any import that pulls in large deps.

## D1 Database
- Rule 5: D1 uses SQLite semantics, not Postgres. `RETURNING` clause unavailable
  in D1 bindings prior to 2024. Use `.run()` then a follow-up `.first()` to return inserted rows.
- Rule 6: Multi-statement transactions require `db.batch([...])`, not sequential awaits.
- Rule 7: D1 `prepare().bind()` — parameters are 1-indexed (`?1`, `?2`), not `$1`/`$2`.
- Rule 8: Never use `SELECT *` in D1 production queries — column drift causes silent failures.
```

Then add a `@cloudflare-rules.md` reference to the project's CLAUDE.md (or to the developer agent for that project) so it's loaded as a required pre-flight check.

**Impact:** Reduces capability_gaps and fix_attempts in cloudflare-workers/d1 sessions by giving the developer agent the same runtime-specific awareness that `.claude/rules/xano-rules.md` provides for Mirror. The 15-rule Mirror pattern reduced friction on Xano endpoints to near-zero after the file was established.

**Risk:** Medium. Rules must be verified against the actual Cloudflare Workers/D1 versions in use — some rules (especially D1 `RETURNING` behavior) changed across D1 beta versions. Incorrect rules are worse than no rules. Mitigation: mark each rule with a version note and add `[VERIFY AGAINST LIVE ENDPOINT]` for any D1 behavioral claim before committing the file.

## Proposal Track Record
36 total, 21 accepted, 6 pending

## Benchmarks
| Benchmark | Status | Detail |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| Friction Rate | WATCH | 3 fix_attempts (14d) |
| Proposal Hit Rate | 67% | 21/31 accepted |
| Eval Signal | OK | 3 consecutive all-pass runs |

## Action Required: NO
